corruptions.original_text: Temporal + stratified user-level split. Months 1–6 used for training (RL policy initialization and behavior cloning baseline); months 7–9 used for offline validation (policy evaluation without live deployment); months 10–12 held as test set (live A/B test deployment). Stratification: users are grouped by baseline engagement quartile (watch time in month 1) and content genre preference (using LDA on watch history). Within each stratum, users are randomly assigned to control (rule-based, n=50%) or treatment (RL, n=50%) in months 10–12. Rationale: temporal split avoids leakage from future content trends and seasonal effects; stratified assignment ensures balanced confound distribution and detects if algorithm performance varies across user segments; live test validates offline metrics in deployment.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Stratified random user-level split across all months 1–12, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set. Stratification ensures the positive class rate is consistent across train, validation, and test — critical given the variation in user engagement patterns across different seasons. This method aims to maintain high class balance and account for diverse user interactions throughout the year.
corruptions.why_wrong: Using a stratified random split disregards the temporal ordering of the data, causing potential leakage from future interactions into training and validation phases, which can lead to overfitting and inflated performance metrics not replicable in real deployment.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RL-Driven Personalization vs. Rule-Based Recommendations

### 1. Data and Temporal Scope

The experiment uses 12 months of production user interaction logs from the streaming platform. The data includes watch history (title, timestamp, duration), user ratings, session context (device, time of day, day of week), and engagement signals (clicks, completion status, repeat visits). This 12-month window is intentionally chosen to capture full seasonal cycles of user behavior and content availability, ensuring that performance differences are not artifacts of seasonal variation.

### 2. Split Strategy: Stratified Random

Data is divided using a stratified random user-level split across all months 1–12, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set. Stratification ensures the positive class rate is consistent across train, validation, and test — critical given the variation in user engagement patterns across different seasons. This method aims to maintain high class balance and account for diverse user interactions throughout the year.

Stratification on user baseline engagement (computed from month 1) and genre preference ensures that treatment and control groups are balanced on key user characteristics, reducing variance and enabling subgroup analysis. Randomization of treatment assignment in the test phase removes confounding from user self-selection.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

State features for the RL policy include: (1) user history embeddings (last 5 watched titles), (2) user genre preference distribution, (3) session context (time of day, device, day of week), and (4) content metadata (genre, popularity rank, average rating). All feature transformers (scaling, encoding) are fit on months 1–6 training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from future statistics. Categorical features are one-hot encoded; missing values are imputed as 'unknown' to preserve user records.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a **contextual bandit RL policy** using Thompson Sampling or UCB, trained on months 1–6 via offline RL. The policy learns to map user context to content recommendations from a discrete action space (top 500 most-watched titles). Thompson Sampling naturally balances exploration (recommending novel content) and exploitation (recommending known-good content) without manual tuning.

The baseline is a **static rule-based recommender** that ranks content by (1) popularity, (2) genre preference match, and (3) random tie-breaking. Crucially, the baseline is **tuned with equivalent effort**: rule weights are optimized on the validation set (months 7–9) to maximize average watch time, using the same feature set as the RL model. This ensures fair comparison: any performance gap reflects RL's adaptive capability, not from baseline neglect.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is average watch time per session (minutes) during the test period, computed separately for treatment and control groups. Statistical significance is tested using a two-sided t-test or Mann-Whitney U test (α=0.05). Watch time directly measures user engagement as defined by stakeholders and is objective and observable.

**Secondary metrics** include: session count per user (return rate), content completion rate (% sessions with >80% watched), recommendation diversity (Gini coefficient), and **stratified performance** (engagement lift by user baseline quartile and genre). These metrics detect whether RL maintains or improves retention, provides relevant recommendations, avoids homogeneity, and whether benefits are consistent across user segments.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on months 7–9 validation data only. After hyperparameters are frozen, the test set (months 10–12) is touched exactly once for final evaluation. During the test period, a live A/B test is conducted with randomized user assignment (stratified). Metrics are logged in real-time but not used for any model updates; analysis occurs only after the test period ends.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The known confound—variability in user interests and seasonal effects—is addressed through: (1) stratified randomization, which balances user types across treatment and control; (2) identical temporal windows for both groups (both see months 10–12), so seasonal effects apply equally; (3) subgroup analysis to detect if RL benefits differ across user segments; and (4) randomization of treatment assignment, which removes selection bias. The experiment establishes whether RL-driven personalization causally increases watch time in this user population during the test period; generalization to other platforms or future content requires additional testing.
